course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Linear Transformations and Standard Matrices
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to build standard matrices, compute transformations, and connect one-to-one/onto with pivots and matrix shape.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with: a linear transformation is controlled by what it does to basis vectors.
Remind students that columns are images of basis vectors.
Do Problem 2 to connect transformations with kernel/range.
Do Problem 3 to show how to use nonstandard input vectors.
sayThisFirst: A linear transformation is a function that respects adding and scaling.
The standard matrix is built from where the standard basis vectors go.
The columns of the matrix are T(e1), T(e2), and so on.
Kernel means inputs that go to zero.
Range means outputs the transformation can hit.
boardPlan: Write A=[T(e1) T(e2) ...] at the top.
Draw a small before/after picture: input vector goes in, output vector comes out.
Problem 1: build a 2 by 2 matrix from T(e1), T(e2).
Problem 2: find kernel and range for a map R3 to R2.
Problem 3: use linearity when the given vectors are not e1 and e2.

keyFormulas.name: Standard matrix
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: The columns are where the standard basis vectors land.

keyFormulas.name: Kernel
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Kernel is the set of inputs that get sent to zero.

keyFormulas.name: One-to-one
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Only the zero input goes to zero.

commonMistakes: Putting T(e1) and T(e2) as rows instead of columns.
Saying a transformation from R3 to R2 can be one-to-one without checking pivots.
Forgetting that linearity lets you break T(au+bv) into aT(u)+bT(v).
notationWarnings: Use T(e_1), T(e_2), not Te1, when writing transformation values.
Write \ker(T) for kernel and \operatorname{range}(T) for range.
When using a matrix, write T(x)=Ax to connect the function and the matrix.

problems.title: Build the standard matrix from basis vector images
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let \(T:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2\) be linear with
\[T(e_1)=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\end{bmatrix},\qquad T(e_2)=\begin{bmatrix}3\\4\end{bmatrix}.\]
Find the standard matrix for \(T\), compute \(T(5,-2)\), and decide whether \(T\) is one-to-one and onto.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: the columns are the images of the standard basis vectors. This is the main idea.

drawInstructions: Draw e1 and e2 in the input plane.
Draw their images T(e1) and T(e2) in the output plane.
Write the output vectors as columns of A.
Show that det(A)=11, so the map does not collapse area to zero.

walkthrough.label: Check one-to-one and onto
walkthrough.latex: \[\det(A)=2(4)-3(-1)=11\neq 0.\]\nSo A is invertible. Since this is a map \(\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2\), the transformation is both one-to-one and onto.

commonMistakes: Putting the two image vectors as rows.
Forgetting the determinant test only works this cleanly here because the matrix is square.
Saying onto because there are two outputs, instead of checking pivots/invertibility.

practiceLabPointer.note: More transformation and standard matrix practice.

problems.title: Kernel and range of a transformation
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Let \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2\) be
\[T(x,y,z)=\begin{bmatrix}x-2y+z\\3x+y\end{bmatrix}.\]
Find the standard matrix, a basis for \(\ker(T)\), and the range of \(T\). Is \(T\) one-to-one? Is it onto?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: kernel means set the output equal to zero. Range means look at pivot columns / output space.

drawInstructions: Draw a box labeled input R^3 and a box labeled output R^2.
Draw a line through the origin in R^3 labeled kernel direction (1,-3,-7).
Draw the whole output plane labeled range = R^2.
Say: a nonzero kernel means not one-to-one; full output plane means onto.

walkthrough.label: Find the kernel
walkthrough.latex: Set \(T(x,y,z)=0\):\n\[x-2y+z=0,\qquad 3x+y=0.\]\nFrom the second equation, \(y=-3x\). Substitute into the first: \(x+6x+z=0\), so \(z=-7x\).

walkthrough.label: Find the range
walkthrough.latex: The two rows are independent, so the rank is 2. Since the output space is \(\mathbb{R}^2\), rank 2 means \(\operatorname{range}(T)=\mathbb{R}^2\).

walkthrough.label: Decide one-to-one and onto
walkthrough.latex: The kernel contains nonzero vectors, so \(T\) is not one-to-one. The range is all of \(\mathbb{R}^2\), so \(T\) is onto.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: A=\begin{bmatrix}1&-2&1\\3&1&0\end{bmatrix},\quad \ker(T)=\operatorname{span}\{(1,-3,-7)\},\quad \operatorname{range}(T)=\mathbb{R}^2.\; T\text{ is onto but not one-to-one.}
commonMistakes: Setting the input equal to zero instead of the output when finding kernel.
Saying not onto just because the domain has three variables.
Forgetting that a nonzero kernel means not one-to-one.

practiceLabPointer.note: More practice with kernels, images, and transformation matrices.

problems.title: Using linearity with nonstandard input vectors
skills: Express a vector as a combination
Find transformation values from given data
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: we are not given T(e1) directly. First write (3,5) using the vectors we do know.

drawInstructions: Draw u=(1,2), v=(0,1), and target (3,5) in the input plane.
Draw the output as a vector in R3 only conceptually; exact 3D drawing is not necessary.

walkthrough.label: Write the target input as a combination
walkthrough.latex: We want constants a and b with\n\[a(1,2)+b(0,1)=(3,5).\]\nFrom the first coordinate, \(a=3\). Then \(2a+b=5\), so \(6+b=5\), and \(b=-1\).

commonMistakes: Trying to plug (3,5) into a formula that was never given.
Forgetting the negative sign when using -T(0,1).
Assuming the given input vectors are e1 and e2. They are not.

practiceLabPointer.note: More practice with linearity and standard matrices.

wrapUpQuestions: What goes in the columns of a standard matrix?
What does a nonzero kernel tell us?
How can we use linearity when the input is written as a combination?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 4
linksToWebsite.reason: More practice with transformations, determinants, and inverses.

qualityControlNotes: Problem 2 checked: kernel basis (1,-3,-7) satisfies both equations; rank is 2 so range is R^2.
reviewSummary: Session 07 emphasizes columns-as-images and very simple kernel/range language. All computations were checked.
